title: Vulnerability rediscovery after patch — regressed exposures
exclusions: Does NOT root-cause the regression; hands off to remediation with evidence.
evidence: Daily 'vuln_regression' captures reappearing CVEs and writes them to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Part of RA-5 is verifying remediation effectiveness — the UC catches patch regressions that would otherwise be silent.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 RA-5 (Vulnerability scanning) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.43.2: Vulnerability rediscovery after patch — regressed exposures.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.43.2' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI 6.3 requires proactive verification; regressed vulns defeat the remediation.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that PCI DSS 6.3 (Vulnerabilities identified and addressed) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.43.2: Vulnerability rediscovery after patch — regressed exposures.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.43.2' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When a CVE previously marked remediated on a host reappears in a subsequent scan, the UC fires with host, cve, remediated_at, rediscovered_at.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Remediated CVEs that remain undetected in subsequent scans do NOT fire.

spl:
index=vuln earliest=-90d | sort 0 host cve _time | streamstats latest(action) as last_action by host cve
| where last_action="remediated"
| join host cve [search index=vuln action=detected earliest=-7d]
| table host cve remediated_at _time as rediscovered_at
description: Catches situations where a patched vulnerability resurfaces — typically due to image rollback, snapshot restore, or patch-management regression. Every reappearance erodes prior compliance evidence.
value: Prevents the compliance report from overstating the remediation rate by double-counting recurring findings.
implementation: (1) Maintain history of vulnerability state per host/CVE; (2) run daily; (3) on finding, re-open original remediation ticket. Indexes required: `index=vuln`.
visualization: Table of regressions, trend of regressions per scan cycle, single-value 'patches holding (30d)'. Key fields: `cve`, `host`, `rediscovered_at`, `remediated_at`.
knownFalsePositives: SLA breaches during compatibility testing, vendor lag, or planned change windows.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: See subcategory preamble..
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain history of vulnerability state per host/CVE; (2) run daily; (3) on finding, re-open original remediation ticket.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=vuln earliest=-90d | sort 0 host cve _time | streamstats latest(action) as last_action by host cve
| where last_action="remediated"
| join host cve [search index=vuln action=detected earliest=-7d]
| table host cve remediated_at _time as rediscovered_at
```

Understanding this SPL

**Vulnerability rediscovery after patch — regressed exposures** — Prevents the compliance report from overstating the remediation rate by double-counting recurring findings.

Documented **Data sources**: See subcategory preamble. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: vuln.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=vuln, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
• `streamstats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host cve** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Filters the current rows with `where last_action="remediated"` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Correlates data from a subsearch to enrich the primary result set with additional context.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.

Output fields: _time, cve, host, rediscovered_at, remediated_at.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare results with the audit register and the GRC system so evidence lines up with control owners, attestations, and retention policy.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table of regressions, trend of regressions per scan cycle, single-value 'patches holding (30d)'.
grandmaExplanation: We help you see patch timing against your service promises so you can explain delays with evidence.
